topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt like sandpaper under my fingertips as I unfolded the document. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air that seemed to snag in my throat. The typeface was elegant, almost mocking in its formality, detailing my life—my childhood dreams, my career trajectory, even the precise wording of my most cherished memories. Every sentence was a stranger’s interpretation, a narrative stitched together from fragments I didn't recognize. My hands began to tremble uncontrollably, the edges of the paper blurring before my eyes. I dropped it onto the mahogany desk with a startling clatter, the sound echoing unnervingly loud in the silence of the study. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the room, running a hand through my already disheveled hair. "No," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy. "This isn't right. This is not me." A cold dread began to coil in my stomach, tightening with every frantic step.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the illuminated text mocking me from the monitor. It was my life story, meticulously chronicled, complete with anecdotes I’d never shared, professional milestones I’d never achieved. My jaw locked so tight I could feel the muscles ache. I leaned closer, scrutinizing the dates, the locations, the emotional undercurrents woven into the prose. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision swim momentarily. I slapped my palm against the desk, a desperate attempt to anchor myself to reality, but the surface felt slick and unyielding. I backed away slowly, my heels scraping against the polished floorboards. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat demanding release. I started breathing in short, ragged gasps, trying to force the swirling confusion to settle, but the information only seemed to churn faster, pulling me under.

[story 3]

The envelope had been tucked inside a book I hadn't opened in years, a forgotten relic from a trip abroad. When I finally pulled it out, the contents spilled onto the worn carpet. The script inside was flawless, intimate, and devastatingly accurate in ways that felt invasive. My fingers fumbled, trying to decipher the flow of the narrative, but the words refused to cohere into anything familiar. A sudden chill swept through the room, raising gooseflesh along my arms. I dropped to my knees, my entire body rigid with tension. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to block out the sheer volume of fabricated history. When I finally forced my eyes open, I looked wildly around the empty room, searching for an escape route that didn't exist. A low, involuntary moan escaped my lips as the implications of what I was reading settled over me, heavy and suffocating.
